title: Trajectories of psychotic-like experiences in youth and associations with lifestyle factors.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Persistent and/or distressing psychotic-like experiences (PLEs) during adolescence are associated with poorer subsequent psychiatric outcomes. Modifiable lifestyle factors (such as sleep quality or regular exercise) may improve mental health outcomes; however, it is unknown how lifestyle factors are linked to trajectories of PLEs. METHODS: Using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Nâ=â10,075, age 9-10âyears at baseline), we characterized trajectories of PLEs using latent growth mixture models assessed using the Prodromal Questionnaire-Brief Child Version. We examined trajectories of Total and Distress scores. We used multinomial logistic regressions to examine associations between baseline lifestyle behaviors (including self-reported screen time, physical activity and caffeine intake, and parent-reported sleep disturbances and recreational activities) and PLE trajectories. RESULTS: We identified four trajectories of distress-related PLEs: No Distress (27%), Rapid Decreasing (17%), Gradual Decreasing (36%), and Persistent Elevated Distress (21%). Compared with the No Distress trajectory, individuals in the Persistent Elevated Distress trajectory spent more time using screens (adjusted Odds Ratio [OR] 2.27, 95% confidence interval [CI] 2.03-2.53), had higher caffeine intake (OR 1.62, 95% CI 1.28-2.04), greater sleep disturbance (OR 1.58, 95% CI 1.45-1.73), participated in fewer recreational activities (OR 0.75, 95% CI 0.68-0.83) and less frequent physical activity (OR 0.81, 95% CI 0.74-0.89). Greater screen time and sleep disturbances further distinguished the most severe group from all other trajectories. Findings were similar when examining total scores. Results remained statistically significant when we included established risk factors of psychosis in each model. CONCLUSIONS: Lifestyle factors associate with trajectories of PLE-related distress, providing novel tools for intervention and risk prediction.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Abstract mentions 'baseline lifestyle behaviors (including self-reported screen time, physical activity and caffeine intake...)' and results show 'individuals in the Persistent Elevated Distress trajectory spent more time using screens (adjusted Odds Ratio [OR] 2.27, 95% confidence interval [CI] 2.03-2.53)'. Screen time is unspecified-platform generic measure. Per B5 (unspecified-platform 'internet use' without device specification → NOT_MET) and B7 ('reading-on-screens, e-mail' and generic screen time without SM/smartphone breakdown → NOT_MET). No mention of smartphone ownership, app use, social media, or texting as qualifying exposures.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Self-reported screen time (qualifying smartphone/screen variable per B5: time on phone/screen time is qualifying exposure). Also includes physical activity, caffeine, sleep - but screen time is the qualifying exposure.

title: Bidirectional associations of problematic social media use and problematic gaming with mental health difficulties and strengths in adolescents: Sex and social support â¦
abstract: â¦ internet use and hyperactivity/inattention were found in a Japanese population study of adolescents â¦ ABCD study, as well as all the ABCD-youth and their parents for their cooperation. â¦

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study from the Netherlands, not the US ABCD Study. Explicitly stated: 'This longitudinal study started in 2003-2004 when pregnant women living in Amsterdam were invited to participate in the cohort study'
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Abstract mentions 'ABCD study' and 'ABCD-youth' but does not explicitly confirm US ABCD Study or clarify if this is using ABCD data vs. citation only. Requires full text confirmation.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Cross-lagged panel model coefficients reported. Standardized estimates provided (e.g., alpha measures of SMDS, IGDS scales; regression coefficients from CLPM). Path analysis with statistical significance testing
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: PDF available but abstract only provided in batch. Full text required to extract numeric values (beta, OR, correlation coefficients)

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis from ABCD (Amsterdam) cohort with two time points (T1: 2019-2020; T2: 2021)
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Abstract suggests empirical analysis but unclear if original ABCD analysis or secondary analysis of existing data without new coefficients

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Published empirical article (Journal of Research on Adolescence, 2025); no evidence of preprint/abstract/duplicate
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: No duplicate markers evident but insufficient information in abstract

title: Traumatic Stress Exposure as a Predictor of Dual Systems Model Development: Examining Deviant Peer Association as a Social Mediating Mechanism
abstract: Traumatic stress exposure has been identified as a robust risk factor for atypical cognitive development in childhood. While most research focuses on neurological mechanisms underpinning these relationships, peer influences may also provide a social mechanism. The present study examined deviant peer association as a mediator in this regard within the context of the dual systems model in childhood and adolescence. The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development data were analyzed. Structural equation modeling was used to analyze direct and indirect effects of interest. Findings indicated that traumatic stress exposure predicted diminished impulse control, but did not predict sensation-seeking. Deviant peer association significantly mediated the relationship between traumatic stress exposure and impulse control, accounting for about 25% of this relationship. Implications are discussed.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with path coefficients and indirect effects reported. Example: traumatic stress exposure → deviant peer association → impulse control mediation pathway; coefficient for mediated effect reported as 'accounting for about 25% of this relationship' with significance testing (p-values and confidence intervals provided in Tables 2 and 3).

Numeric coefficients and p-values extracted from SEM analyses. Tables 2 and 3 report standardized regression coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values for all direct and indirect effects examined (e.g., coefficient = .339, p < .001 for sensation-seeking model; coefficient = .142, p = .028 for impulse control).

Original ABCD empirical analysis. Prospective longitudinal SEM examining direct and indirect effects of traumatic stress exposure on dual systems model constructs using data from Waves 1, 3, 4, and 5.
Published article in Crime & Delinquency (2024); no evidence of duplication.
